title: The King of Glory
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 04-02-23 - The King of Glory - Matthew 21_1-17 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Deliberate Staging of the Entry
subpoints: Matthew follows Jesus's Galilean ministry and marks this as the climactic entrance into Jerusalem; since chapter 16, he has been preparing readers for what happens there

From Jericho, Jesus healed two blind men in view of Passover pilgrims already convinced he might be the Messiah; the raising of Lazarus (John 11) days earlier had tipped the religious authorities into plotting his death
Jesus intentionally staged this entrance to fulfill prophecy, to declare his messiahship publicly, and to open the door of his own death on his own timetable
Matthew's purpose: to show that every detail fulfills OT prophecy — Zechariah 9:9 is quoted explicitly

outline.title: 1. Public Declaration of Messiahship
subpoints: Previously Jesus had often told people to keep quiet about his identity; no more — he openly receives the crowd's worship and lets them shout 'Hosanna to the Son of David' at the top of their lungs
When the Pharisees demanded silence, Jesus said: if they stop, the very stones will cry out
He rides on an unbroken colt — the only time in all the Gospels that Jesus rides an animal; a deliberate, public, unmistakable claim to kingship
Everything is orchestrated: the specific animal, the disciples' retrieval, the crowd, the timing — Jesus is daring the religious authorities to do something about it; his hour has come

outline.title: 2. The King Who Is Not What the People Expected
subpoints: The crowd expected a military Messiah to liberate them from Rome; they quoted Psalm 118, a Messianic psalm, with that hope in mind — 'we were hoping that it was he who was going to redeem Israel' (Luke 24:21)
He rides not on a warhorse but on a donkey's foal — 'gentle and lowly,' as he had described himself earlier in Matthew
Luke tells us that as the crowd praised him, Jesus was weeping over Jerusalem (Luke 19:41-44) — prophesying its destruction in 70 AD because it did not know 'the time of its visitation'
His crown would be thorns; his throne a cross; his royal robe the guilt of our sins; the same crowd that cried 'Hosanna!' on Sunday cried 'Crucify him!' on Friday

outline.title: 3. Application: What Kind of King Are You Looking For?
subpoints: Multitudes today welcome Jesus as a health-and-prosperity King; they want peace, happiness, and personal success — and are prepared to shout Hosanna as long as they get it
But Jesus did not come to deliver us from Rome or poverty first; he came to give us what we most need: forgiveness of sin, peace with God, eternal life

For those who know themselves sinners, who know God to be holy, who know eternal life to be more important than anything in this world — Jesus on his donkey is a king they will follow with glad hearts
The question the city asked still stands: 'Who is this?' (v.10) — everything depends on the answer

summary: A Palm Sunday sermon on Matthew 21:1-17. Matthew has been building toward Jerusalem since chapter 16; by the time of this entrance, the raising of Lazarus (John 11) had already pushed the authorities to plot Jesus's death. The triumphal entry was fully orchestrated by Jesus — he deliberately staged a public declaration of messiahship, fulfilling Zechariah 9:9, riding an unbroken colt (the only time in the Gospels he rides an animal), and openly receiving worship he had previously muted. But the entry also declared that he was not the Messiah the people expected: not a warhorse conqueror but a humble king riding a donkey's foal. Luke records that even as the crowd praised him, Jesus wept over Jerusalem, prophesying its destruction in 70 AD. His throne would be a cross; his crown, thorns. The sermon closes with the question: what kind of king are you looking for? Those who know themselves sinners will welcome the king who comes not to impress with power but to love at great cost.
transcript:
## Introduction

We are going to read from Matthew chapter 21, verses 1 through 11. This is the word of our Lord.

*Now when they drew near Jerusalem and came to Bethphage at the Mount of Olives, Jesus sent two disciples, saying to them, 'Go into the village opposite you, and immediately you will find a donkey tied, and a colt with her. Loose them and bring them to me. And if anyone says anything to you, you shall say, "The Lord has need of them," and immediately he will send them.' All this was done that it might be fulfilled which was spoken by the prophet, saying: 'Tell the daughter of Zion, "Behold, your King is coming to you, lowly, and sitting on a donkey, a colt, the foal of a donkey."' So the disciples went and did as Jesus commanded them. They brought the donkey and the colt, laid their clothes on them, and set him on them. And a very great multitude spread their clothes on the road; others cut down branches from the trees and spread them on the road. Then the multitudes who went before and those who followed cried out, saying: 'Hosanna to the Son of David! Blessed is he who comes in the name of the LORD! Hosanna in the highest!' And when he had come into Jerusalem, all the city was moved, saying, 'Who is this?' So the multitudes said, 'This is Jesus, the prophet from Nazareth of Galilee.'*

This passage marks the beginning of what has traditionally been called Holy Week or Passion Week.

Matthew has not recorded Jesus's previous visits to Jerusalem. John, for example, records at least three earlier visits. Matthew is concerned with the Galilean ministry — but since chapter 16, verse 21, he has been preparing his readers for the Lord's arrival in Jerusalem and for the events that take place there.

## The Context

From chapter 19, verse 1, Matthew follows Jesus on his journey from Galilee south through the Transjordan toward Jerusalem. Luke describes this entire period as Jesus 'setting his face toward Jerusalem' — even when he turned away from it geographically, he was heading there in purpose.

On the way, Jesus had healed two blind men in Jericho in full view of crowds of Passover pilgrims. That miracle stirred messianic conviction. And just a few days before this Sunday — John 11 — Jesus had raised Lazarus from the dead. The raising of Lazarus was the tipping point for the religious authorities: from that moment on, they formulated a plan to kill Jesus at the earliest opportunity. Think of the blindness of that — a man raised from the dead after four days, and the response is to kill the one who did it. That is the darkness of the human heart apart from the Spirit.

So as that Sunday morning dawned, the city was buzzing with excited talk about Jesus of Nazareth. And as he rode toward the city on the road from Bethany — surrounded by his disciples, the crowds of pilgrims, those who had come out from the city to meet him — Jesus intentionally staged a grand entrance.

Notice verse 2: Jesus had walked all the way from Galilee. He did not need the donkey for the last mile. The riding of the donkey here is the only record in the entire Gospels of Jesus riding an animal on land. It was a deliberate action. He rode into Jerusalem in order to fulfill the prophecies concerning the Messiah.

## 1. The Public Declaration

For much of his ministry, Jesus had asked people to keep quiet about his identity. That changes here. He openly receives the crowd's adoration. He allows them to spread their garments on the road before him. He lets them shout 'Hosanna to the Son of David' at the tops of their lungs.

When the Pharisees demanded he silence them, he said: if they stop, the very stones will cry out.

Jesus came to Jerusalem on this day to declare — publicly, unmistakably, before witnesses — that he is the Messiah. There is no more staying in the countryside, no more asking for quiet. He is walking into Jerusalem, riding into Jerusalem as the King of kings. His hour has come. And in coming this way, he is deliberately daring the religious authorities to act. He is, as it were, opening the door of his own death — on his own timetable, on his own terms.

John's Gospel tells us plainly: no one takes my life from me; I lay it down of my own accord. Every detail of this entry — the specific animal, the disciples' retrieval, the words to say to the owners — Jesus orchestrated it all.

## 2. The King Who Was Not What They Expected

But the entry also declared that this king would not do what his people wanted him to do.

The crowd was ready to proclaim him king. They quoted Psalm 118, a recognized Messianic psalm. They were thinking: the day has arrived. He is going to lead us against Rome. He is going to restore Israel.

They were right that the day had arrived. They were wrong about what was going to happen.

He did not ride on a warhorse. He did not ride at the head of a column of armed infantry. He rode on a donkey's foal — and as Matthew points out, Zechariah 9 had said exactly that: *lowly, and riding on a donkey.* He had already described himself as 'gentle and lowly in heart' (Matthew 11:29). This is the king you get: gentle, humble, riding a baby donkey.

And as the crowd praised him, Luke tells us, Jesus wept (Luke 19:41-44). Convulsive, heaving tears — over the city he was riding into. 'If you had known, even you, in this your day, the things that make for your peace! But now they are hidden from your eyes.' He prophesied the destruction of Jerusalem — enemies surrounding it, tearing it down, not leaving one stone on another — because it did not know the time of its visitation. Fulfilled in 70 AD.

His people were expecting him to do great things for them. He was weeping because he knew what was coming: his coronation — but with a crown of thorns, a throne that was a cross, and a royal robe that was the guilt of their sins.

## 3. Who Is This?

When Jesus entered the city, all Jerusalem was stirred and people asked: *Who is this?*

That question stands over all of history.

Multitudes today are prepared to welcome Jesus with Hosanna — as long as he will make them healthy, wealthy, and successful. There are still disciples who, like the Twelve on that day, are sure Jesus is going to give them what they want from life. The suffering King — the king who weeps, who is humiliated, scourged, and executed — they don't want that.

But friend, if you think you can handle the salvation part yourself and just need some help with life from Jesus — you are looking for a savior you invented. You are heading in a direction that ends in disaster.

Jesus didn't come primarily to give us better marriages, bigger houses, healthier bodies, or prosperity. He came to give us what we most desperately need and cannot provide ourselves: forgiveness of sins, peace with God, and eternal life.

For those who know themselves sinners — who know God is holy, who know that eternal life is incomparably more important than any earthly prosperity — Jesus on his donkey is a King they will follow. They will put their confidence in him. They will worship him with glad hearts.

For them, the answer to the question is settled: this is the Lord. Blessed is he who comes in the name of the LORD.

Father, thank you for the Lord Jesus Christ — for his humble entrance into Jerusalem, for the cross to which it led, for the crown that was thorns and the throne that was a cross. Help us to cling to him. In his name, amen.
